primary_diagnosis.name: Large local reaction to an insect (arthropod) bite

differential.diagnosis: large local reaction to an insect bite
differential.evidence_basis: Sudden pruritic firm wheal with a central punctum minutes after a bite while gardening — the classic picture of a large local hypersensitivity reaction to arthropod saliva.

Redness, induration and warmth can overlap; must be actively excluded because it needs antibiotics — but the hours-not-days timeline, itch over pain, single punctum and absence of fever/lymphangitis argue strongly against it.
The patient's own fear; must be screened for and excluded, but there are no airway, breathing, circulatory or generalised urticarial features.
Outdoor exposure keeps tick bite possible, but there is no embedded tick and a rapidly-forming firm wheal is not the slowly-expanding erythema migrans of Lyme disease.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the leading benign diagnosis and the two must-not-miss mimics (cellulitis, anaphylaxis) should all be on the list; the skill is screening the mimics down rather than either anchoring on the patient's panic or dismissing the redness.

differential.diagnosis: large local reaction to an insect bite
differential.evidence_basis: Negative systemic-allergy screen (DI-003) and negative infection/lymphangitis screen (DI-004) leave a benign localised hypersensitivity reaction as the confident clinical diagnosis.
Downgraded but not deleted — early cellulitis cannot be fully excluded remotely, so it is carried into safety-netting rather than treated now.
No airway, breathing, circulatory or generalised urticarial involvement on direct questioning — anaphylaxis is excluded for the current encounter.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the diagnosis is a benign large local reaction; cellulitis stays only as a safety-net contingency.

differential.diagnosis: large local reaction to an insect bite (benign, self-limiting)
differential.evidence_basis: Classic constellation with all dangerous mimics screened negative; management is symptomatic with conservative safety-netting.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final answer is a benign localised bite reaction managed in the community — the correct disposition is low-tier, not emergency.

red_flags.description: Systemic allergic / anaphylactic features — breathing difficulty, facial/lip/tongue swelling, throat tightness, generalised hives, or faintness/dizziness
red_flags.clinical_significance: If present, this converts a benign local reaction into a life-threatening emergency requiring adrenaline and an ambulance.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A developing systemic reaction that is not screened for or safety-netted could progress to airway compromise or shock before help is sought.

red_flags.description: Spreading bacterial infection — expanding erythema, a red streak tracking up the limb (lymphangitis), increasing pain overtaking the itch, fever or chills, or feeling systemically unwell
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals evolving cellulitis requiring prompt medical review and antibiotics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated spreading cellulitis can progress to systemic sepsis.

red_flags.description: Features suggesting significant envenomation or tissue necrosis — a rapidly blistering, darkening or necrotic lesion, severe worsening pain, or systemic toxicity
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would raise concern for a medically significant venomous bite needing urgent in-person assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed recognition of necrotic/systemic envenomation risks progressive tissue damage.

pathophysiology_summary: Salivary antigens injected during an arthropod bite trigger a local IgE-mediated and cell-mediated hypersensitivity response. Mast-cell degranulation releases histamine and other mediators, producing a firm indurated wheal, surrounding erythema, warmth and intense pruritus — a 'large local reaction'. It is benign and self-limiting, typically settling over hours to a few days, and is distinguished from cellulitis (bacterial, days-timeline, pain-predominant, spreading, systemic features) and from systemic anaphylaxis (multi-system, airway/circulatory involvement). Treatment is symptomatic: oral antihistamines to block the histaminergic itch and a topical corticosteroid to suppress the local inflammatory infiltrate, with avoidance of scratching to prevent secondary bacterial infection.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Affective anchoring on the patient's fear
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being pulled into the patient's spider-bite/anaphylaxis panic and over-investigating or over-triaging a benign localised reaction.
Dismissing all erythema and warmth as benign without screening for spreading redness, lymphangitis, pain-predominance and fever — and without safety-netting for it.
Sending a well patient with a benign self-limiting bite reaction to ED or advising 000 when community symptomatic management with safety-netting is correct.
Prescribing oral antibiotics for an uninfected bite reaction — an error of commission and poor antimicrobial stewardship.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of the true firmness, depth and margins of the induration
Objective vital signs (temperature, blood pressure, heart rate, oxygen saturation) without a home device
Auscultation of the chest and examination of the oropharynx to formally exclude airway involvement
Whether early cellulitis is developing beneath the visible surface
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: No emergency escalation is required now. Manage symptomatically (oral antihistamine + topical corticosteroid + wound hygiene) and provide clear, conservative safety-netting: 000 for any evolving anaphylaxis, and prompt/urgent review for any spreading infection.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the benign diagnosis (the constellation is classic). The real risks are (1) over-triage driven by the patient's anxiety, and (2) failing to safety-net the two mimics that cannot be fully excluded remotely.
